title: set
description: A collection of unique values, automatically kept sorted: insert, find, count, and erase — with duplicates silently ignored.
objectives: Understand set as a collection of unique, automatically sorted values
Use insert(), find(), count(), and erase()
See that inserting a duplicate has no effect

sections.text: STL Containers previewed set as "a collection of unique values, automatically kept in sorted order." Let's see exactly what that means in practice.
set is a container that stores unique values, automatically kept in sorted order — inserting a value that already exists has no effect.

This is a preview of the range-based for loop, for (int n : numbers), its own upcoming topic — for now, just read it as "for each n in numbers." Notice two things: the output is sorted (10, 20, 30), even though insertion order was 30, 10, 20, 10 — and the second 10 never appears twice, since set silently ignores inserting a value already present.

Recall STL Algorithms' find(): set.find() works the same way conceptually, returning an iterator or end() if not present. count() is especially handy for set specifically, since the answer can only ever be 0 or 1 — there's no way for a duplicate to inflate it.

sections.code:
set<int> numbers = {10, 20, 30};

numbers.erase(20); // removes the value 20 directly, by value — not by position

sections.text: Unlike vector's erase(), which takes an iterator position, set's erase() conveniently accepts the value itself directly.

set is typically implemented as a balanced binary search tree, keeping elements sorted automatically and giving insert(), find(), and erase() all roughly logarithmic-time performance — much faster than scanning a whole vector to check for duplicates or find a value.

sections.title: A guest list where names can't repeat
sections.text: A wedding guest list where every name must be unique, and the final list is printed alphabetically for the door staff, is exactly a set: adding "Aman" a second time changes nothing, and the list is always naturally sorted, no matter what order names were added in.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Expecting set to preserve insertion order — it always keeps elements sorted, regardless of insertion order.
Assuming inserting a duplicate causes an error — it silently does nothing; no exception, no crash.
Using set's erase() the same way as vector's, expecting a position — set's erase() takes the value itself.
Reaching for set when duplicates should actually be kept — a set can never hold duplicate values by definition.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What does set guarantee about its elements?
questions.answer: That every value is unique and that the elements are always kept in sorted order automatically.

questions.question: What happens when you insert a value that's already in the set?
questions.answer: Nothing — the insertion silently has no effect, and the set remains unchanged.

questions.question: How is set typically implemented, and what does that mean for performance?
questions.answer: As a balanced binary search tree, giving insert(), find(), and erase() all roughly logarithmic-time performance.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: set stores unique values, automatically kept in sorted order regardless of insertion order — inserting a duplicate silently has no effect. insert() adds a value, find() and count() check for a value's presence (count() can only ever return 0 or 1, since duplicates are impossible), and erase() removes by value directly rather than by position. It's typically implemented as a balanced binary search tree, giving these operations roughly logarithmic-time performance, much faster than scanning through a vector.

sections.title: Trick to Remember set
sections.text: set = a guest list, no repeat names allowed, always printed alphabetically. Insert a duplicate = nothing happens. count() can only ever be 0 or 1.
